news_summary: Today's space news highlights exciting progress in Mars exploration with new drill samples and climate insights, NASA's team expansion, and groundbreaking discoveries about planet formation around supermassive black holes.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées passionnantes dans l'exploration de Mars, avec de nouveaux échantillons prélevés par forage et de nouvelles données sur le climat, l'élargissement de l'équipe de la NASA, ainsi que des découvertes révolutionnaires sur la formation des planètes autour des trous noirs supermassifs.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan avances emocionantes en la exploración de Marte, con nuevas muestras obtenidas mediante perforación y nuevos datos sobre el clima, la ampliación del equipo de la NASA y descubrimientos revolucionarios sobre la formación de planetas alrededor de agujeros negros supermasivos.

articles.title: Curiosity Blog, Sols 4900-4907: Pasadena, We Have a Drill Sample!
articles.summary: Written by Abigail Fraeman, Deputy Project Scientist at Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of Technology Earth planning date: Friday, May 22, 2026 I spent this past weekend eagerly awaiting the downlink from Mars that would show us the results of Curiosity’s drill attempt at “Campo Marte.” A few weeks ago, when Curiosity drilled the “Atacama” […]
articles.content:
NASA’s Mars rover Curiosity acquired this image, the first color look of the “Campo Marte” drill hole, on May 16, 2026. The rover captured the image using its right Mast Camera (Mastcam) — one of a pair of cameras mounted on the head atop the rover’s mast — on Sol 4897, or Martian day 4,897 of the Mars Science Laboratory mission, at 18:05:49 UTC. NASA/JPL-Caltech/MSSS

Written by Abigail Fraeman, Deputy Project Scientist at Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of Technology

Earth planning date: Friday, May 22, 2026

I spent this past weekend eagerly awaiting the downlink from Mars that would show us the results of Curiosity’s drill attempt at “Campo Marte.” A few weeks ago, when Curiosity drilled the “Atacama” block, it had been quite the surprise to see the post-drill images arrive on Earth that showed the rover picking up the entire Atacama block along with the drill. After freeing ourselves from this pesky passenger, the team carefully assessed all the telemetry and imaging data we had collected to understand why the entanglement happened and to mitigate the chance of it happening again. We concluded it would be ok to try another drill in this general area, and nearby Campo Marte looked like a great target because it had all the right geologic features and was significantly bigger than Atacama. What a delight it was to see images, like the Mastcam shown above, streaming down on Saturday that showed Curiosity had successfully retracted its drill from the rock and collected some sample to analyze this time around!

On Monday, the team looked at the pinches of drilled rock powder, or portions, that we had dropped as a test onto part of Curiosity, an element of our standard post-drilling activities. You can also take a look at what we saw — here’s a picture of the rover before we did anything, and here’s what we saw after we delivered the first portion, and then the second portion. Can you make out the little bit of powder that appears between the sample deliveries? This test is important to make sure we’ll provide good samples to the analytical instruments inside our chassis, CheMin and SAM. Beyond their science operations value, I also love seeing these images because they remind me how powerful our laboratory instruments are. With just a little pinch of powder, no more than tens of milligrams, these laboratories can reveal incredibly detailed information about the composition of Martian rocks and give us huge new insights into the planet’s past climate and habitability.

We concluded the portions from Campo Marte looked similar to the drilled samples we’ve previously analyzed, so we went ahead and delivered one portion to CheMin in Monday’s plan. We use the results from CheMin to tailor our analysis of the samples with SAM, so after we saw the first CheMin results in the middle of the week, we made decisions about how to run SAM and then planned to analyze four portions with that instrument in today’s plan. We think we’ll be nearly out of sample after that, but it’s hard to know for sure (we only drilled to a depth of 28 millimeters here, about 1.1 inches, rather than our usual 35 millimeters, or 1.38 inches). To learn more, in this upcoming weekend’s plan, we'll also repeat the sample drop-off test we did right after drilling, which will show us how many portions were left. We do a ton of testing with Curiosity’s twin drill here on Earth, but it's always insightful to see how our hardware performs on Mars under the unique geologic and environmental conditions of that entirely different world.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a successful Mars rover Curiosity drill sample collection and analysis, providing valuable scientific insights about Mars' geology and past climate. This represents a significant achievement in space exploration with broad scientific and public interest, supported by detailed context about the mission and its impact.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Curiosity rover successfully collects new rock sample on Mars
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Curiosity rover has been exploring Mars since 2012, drilling into rocks to collect samples. These samples help scientists learn about the planet’s geology and past environment.

**What happened** – On May 16, 2026, Curiosity drilled into a rock called “Campo Marte” and successfully collected a sample. After a previous attempt with another rock called “Atacama” caused some problems, the team carefully chose Campo Marte because it had good features and was bigger. The rover’s cameras sent back color images showing the drill hole and the sample collection.

**Impact** – This successful sample means Curiosity can analyze Martian rock powder using its onboard labs, CheMin and SAM. These instruments only need a tiny bit of powder to find out what the rocks are made of and what Mars was like long ago. By studying these samples, scientists can better understand Mars’ climate history and whether it might have supported life.

**What’s next step** – The team will continue analyzing the Campo Marte sample with the rover’s labs. They will also do tests to see how much sample is left. This careful process helps them plan the best way to use the remaining powder for more discoveries.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Curiosity’s new rock sample from Campo Marte offers fresh clues about Mars’ past, helping scientists study the planet’s geology and climate in detail.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le rover Curiosity de la NASA collecte avec succès un nouvel échantillon de roche sur Mars
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le rover Curiosity de la NASA explore Mars depuis 2012, forant des roches pour collecter des échantillons. Ces échantillons aident les scientifiques à mieux comprendre la géologie et l’environnement passé de la planète.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 16 mai 2026, Curiosity a foré une roche appelée « Campo Marte » et a réussi à collecter un échantillon. Après une tentative précédente avec une autre roche, « Atacama », qui avait posé problème, l’équipe a choisi Campo Marte car elle avait de bonnes caractéristiques et était plus grande. Les caméras du rover ont envoyé des images en couleur montrant le trou de forage et la collecte de l’échantillon.

**Impact** – Cet échantillon réussi signifie que Curiosity peut analyser la poudre de roche martienne avec ses laboratoires embarqués, CheMin et SAM. Ces instruments ont besoin d’une toute petite quantité de poudre pour découvrir la composition des roches et ce qu’était Mars il y a longtemps. Étudier ces échantillons permet aux scientifiques de mieux comprendre l’histoire climatique de Mars et si la planète a pu abriter la vie.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe poursuivra l’analyse de l’échantillon Campo Marte avec les laboratoires du rover. Ils effectueront aussi des tests pour savoir combien d’échantillon reste. Ce processus minutieux les aide à planifier la meilleure façon d’utiliser la poudre restante pour faire d’autres découvertes.

**En une phrase** – Le nouvel échantillon de roche de Campo Marte par Curiosity offre de nouvelles pistes pour étudier en détail la géologie et le climat passés de Mars.
articles.personality_title_es: El rover Curiosity de la NASA recoge con éxito una nueva muestra de roca en Marte
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El rover Curiosity de la NASA ha estado explorando Marte desde 2012, perforando rocas para recoger muestras. Estas muestras ayudan a los científicos a entender la geología y el pasado ambiente del planeta.

**Qué pasó** – El 16 de mayo de 2026, Curiosity perforó una roca llamada “Campo Marte” y recogió una muestra con éxito. Después de un intento anterior con otra roca llamada “Atacama” que causó problemas, el equipo eligió Campo Marte porque tenía buenas características y era más grande. Las cámaras del rover enviaron imágenes en color mostrando el agujero de perforación y la recolección de la muestra.

**Impacto** – Esta muestra exitosa significa que Curiosity puede analizar el polvo de roca marciana con sus laboratorios a bordo, CheMin y SAM. Estos instrumentos solo necesitan un poco de polvo para descubrir de qué están hechas las rocas y cómo era Marte en el pasado. Estudiar estas muestras ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor la historia climática de Marte y si pudo haber tenido vida.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo continuará analizando la muestra de Campo Marte con los laboratorios del rover. También harán pruebas para saber cuánta muestra queda. Este proceso cuidadoso les ayuda a planear la mejor manera de usar el polvo restante para más descubrimientos.

**Resumen en una frase** – La nueva muestra de roca de Campo Marte recogida por Curiosity ofrece nuevas pistas para estudiar en detalle la geología y el clima pasados de Marte.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a robotic Mars rover gently extracting a small core sample from a vibrant red rock formation on a dusty Martian landscape under a soft, glowing sky, with delicate pinches of fine rock powder floating subtly nearby, symbolizing careful scientific discovery and exploration.

articles.title: NASA Uses Mineralogical Marker to Understand Ancient Martian Climate
articles.summary: Scientists analyzed 20 Martian samples collected by NASA’s Curiosity Rover and found that differences in hematite crystallite size at varying elevations could serve as a new mineralogical marker for understanding Mars’ ancient climate.
articles.content:
While NASA imagery has shown evidence of ancient rivers and lakes on Mars that transitioned to dry dunes, uncertainty remains over the timing of the environmental changes that may have contributed to these shifts.

Now, data collected by NASA’s Curiosity rover has revealed that individual crystals in the iron oxide hematite can be used as a mineralogical marker of changes to Mars’ ancient climate. Because the shape and structure of these crystallites reflect the conditions – such as temperature and water presence – under which they were formed, they can serve as an indicator of when these changes occurred.

Scientists studied 20 samples collected by Curiosity across various elevations throughout Gale Crater for a paper published Thursday in Science. Gale Crater’s walls reveal Mars’ environmental history layer by layer, with deeper elevations capturing its earliest years. The team analyzed data from the rover’s Chemistry and Minerology (CheMin) instrument and discovered that hematite showed different crystallite sizes at different elevations. They also discovered that goethite, a mineral that typically forms alongside hematite, was absent in samples from lower elevations but still present in samples from higher elevations. This suggests that warm groundwater might have remained for up to 4.7 million years in the deepest layers of Gale Crater and that during much of this time, these long-lived aquifers could have been potentially habitable.

This image shows the 20 Curiosity drill samples from Gale Crater that were analyzed for this study. Credit: NASA/JPL-Caltech/MSSS

“What we found was that warm and wet conditions were present for extended periods in buried rocks, despite Mars’ climate becoming colder,” said Tanya Peretyazhko, co-first author of the study and planetary scientist in the Astromaterials Research and Exploration Science division at NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston. “It means that deep in those rocks, those warmer conditions could have made for habitable conditions for much longer periods of time, provided that other essential factors were present.”

Iron oxides are considered indicators of water activity because they form in its presence. This study shows that hematite can also be a marker of climate changes based on its crystallite sizes and structures, which change under different temperatures. The scientists found that hematite crystallites from higher elevations in Gale Crater were less than 10 nanometers in size, while crystallites from lower locations were generally larger, reaching up to 65 nanometers. These findings aligned with the observations that samples from higher elevations contained both hematite and goethite, while lower elevation samples lacked goethite.

What we found was that warm and wet conditions were present for extended periods in buried rocks, despite Mars’ climate becoming colder." Tanya Peretyazhko Planetary Scientist

They concluded that, under warmer conditions when the pH of water is neutral or slightly alkaline, goethite can transform into hematite. These warmer conditions also favored an increase in hematite crystallite size in the deeper layers of Gale Crater through a process known as Ostwald ripening, in which smaller crystallites dissolve and contribute to the growth of larger ones.

“This can tell you that the top layers were colder and didn’t have enough water, or the water presence was relatively short-lived, so the crystallites didn’t have sufficient time and conditions to grow in size,” said Peretyazhko. “But the lower layers had longstanding warm water that allowed those crystallites to grow.”

An artist rendering of the Curiosity rover with its scientific instruments labeled. Scientists used the Chemistry and Minerology (CheMin) instrument to perform X-ray diffraction analysis on samples of powdered rock. Credit: NASA/JPL-Caltech/MSSS

A unique highlight of this study is that the data comes from Martian samples, rather than from theoretical modeling. Curiosity’s robotic arm delivered powdered rock to CheMin’s input funnel, where it was analyzed. “With CheMin’s X-ray diffraction patterns, we can look at the hematite crystal’s size and dimensions, information that that can’t be gathered from satellite analysis of the Martian surface.” said Tom Bristow, principal investigator of the CheMin instrument at NASA’s Ames Research Center in California’s Silicon Valley.

Ashwin Vasavada, Curiosity’s project scientist at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California, said CheMin is capable of making measurements with extraordinary scientific fidelity.

“It doesn’t just tell you there is hematite," Vasavada explained. "One can use the data to extract the size and shape of the hematite crystallites and the presence of other related minerals, all of which were necessary to produce this result.”

More about Curiosity

Curiosity was built by NASA JPL, which is managed by Caltech in Pasadena, California. NASA JPL leads the mission on behalf of NASA’s Science Mission Directorate in Washington as part of NASA’s Mars Exploration Program portfolio. CheMin, led by NASA Ames , is one of 10 science instruments aboard Curiosity and has a cross-country team of scientists, including researchers at NASA Ames, University of Arizona, California Institute of Technology, Planetary Science Institute, Carnegie Institution for Science, Lunar and Planetary Institute, JPL, NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland, and NASA’s Johnson. The team combines expertise in mineralogy, petrology, materials science, astrobiology and soil science, with experience studying terrestrial, lunar and Martian rocks.

For more information on NASA’s Curiosity rover, visit:

https://science.nasa.gov/mission/msl-curiosity

Karen Fox / Alana Johnson

Headquarters, Washington

240-285-5155 / 202-672-4780

karen.c.fox@nasa.gov / alana.r.johnson@nasa.gov

Victoria Segovia

Johnson Space Center, Houston

281-483-5111

victoria.segovia@nasa.gov
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery using actual Martian samples from NASA's Curiosity rover, revealing new mineralogical markers that improve understanding of Mars' ancient climate and potential habitability. This breakthrough has broad implications for planetary science and astrobiology, providing meaningful insight into Mars' environmental history with substantial context and detailed findings.
articles.personality_title: NASA finds new mineral clues to Mars’ ancient climate using Curiosity rover samples
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists want to understand how Mars’ climate changed long ago, especially when it went from having rivers and lakes to becoming dry. Knowing when these changes happened helps us learn if Mars could have supported life.

**What happened** – NASA’s Curiosity rover collected 20 rock samples from different heights inside Gale Crater on Mars. Researchers studied tiny crystals of a mineral called hematite in these rocks using an instrument called CheMin. They found that the size of hematite crystals changes depending on the rock’s elevation. They also noticed another mineral, goethite, was missing in lower layers but present higher up. These clues suggest warm water existed deep underground for millions of years.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that Mars had long-lasting warm and wet conditions underground, even as the surface became colder. The size of hematite crystals acts like a marker, helping scientists pinpoint when and where these conditions existed. This helps us understand Mars’ climate history better and shows that parts of Mars might have been habitable for a long time.

**What's next step** – Scientists will use this new mineral marker to study other Martian rocks and continue searching for signs of past water and possible life. Future missions might also focus on areas where these minerals appear to learn more about Mars’ environment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By studying hematite crystals in rocks from Mars, NASA scientists uncovered new evidence that warm, wet conditions lasted deep underground for millions of years, improving our understanding of the planet’s ancient climate and habitability.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA découvre de nouveaux indices minéraux sur le climat ancien de Mars grâce aux échantillons du rover Curiosity
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent à comprendre comment le climat de Mars a changé il y a longtemps, notamment quand la planète est passée de rivières et lacs à un désert sec. Savoir quand ces changements sont survenus aide à savoir si Mars a pu abriter la vie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le rover Curiosity de la NASA a recueilli 20 échantillons de roche à différentes hauteurs dans le cratère Gale sur Mars. Les chercheurs ont étudié de minuscules cristaux d’un minéral appelé hématite dans ces roches avec un instrument nommé CheMin. Ils ont découvert que la taille des cristaux d’hématite varie selon l’altitude des roches. Ils ont aussi remarqué qu’un autre minéral, la goethite, était absent dans les couches basses mais présent plus haut. Ces indices suggèrent que de l’eau chaude a existé profondément sous la surface pendant des millions d’années.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que Mars a connu des conditions chaudes et humides souterraines pendant longtemps, même si la surface devenait plus froide. La taille des cristaux d’hématite sert de marqueur, permettant aux scientifiques de déterminer quand et où ces conditions existaient. Cela aide à mieux comprendre l’histoire du climat martien et montre que certaines parties de Mars auraient pu être habitables longtemps.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques utiliseront ce nouveau marqueur minéral pour étudier d’autres roches martiennes et continueront à chercher des signes d’eau passée et de vie possible. Les futures missions pourraient aussi se concentrer sur les zones où ces minéraux apparaissent pour en apprendre davantage sur l’environnement de Mars.

**En une phrase** – En étudiant les cristaux d’hématite dans des roches martiennes, les scientifiques de la NASA ont découvert que des conditions chaudes et humides ont duré des millions d’années sous la surface, améliorant notre compréhension du climat ancien et de l’habitabilité de Mars.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA descubre nuevas pistas minerales sobre el antiguo clima de Marte usando muestras del rover Curiosity
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos quieren entender cómo cambió el clima de Marte hace mucho tiempo, especialmente cuándo pasó de tener ríos y lagos a ser un lugar seco. Saber cuándo ocurrieron estos cambios ayuda a saber si Marte pudo haber tenido vida.

**Qué pasó** – El rover Curiosity de la NASA recogió 20 muestras de roca en diferentes alturas dentro del cráter Gale en Marte. Los investigadores estudiaron pequeños cristales de un mineral llamado hematita en estas rocas usando un instrumento llamado CheMin. Encontraron que el tamaño de los cristales de hematita cambia según la altura de la roca. También notaron que otro mineral, goethita, estaba ausente en las capas bajas pero presente en las altas. Estas pistas sugieren que hubo agua tibia bajo tierra durante millones de años.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que Marte tuvo condiciones cálidas y húmedas duraderas bajo tierra, incluso cuando la superficie se enfriaba. El tamaño de los cristales de hematita funciona como una señal que ayuda a los científicos a saber cuándo y dónde existieron estas condiciones. Esto mejora nuestro conocimiento de la historia climática de Marte y muestra que partes del planeta pudieron haber sido habitables por mucho tiempo.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán este nuevo marcador mineral para estudiar otras rocas marcianas y seguir buscando señales de agua pasada y posible vida. Las futuras misiones también podrían enfocarse en áreas donde aparecen estos minerales para aprender más sobre el ambiente de Marte.

**Una frase clave** – Al estudiar cristales de hematita en rocas de Marte, los científicos de la NASA encontraron que las condiciones cálidas y húmedas duraron millones de años bajo tierra, mejorando nuestra comprensión del antiguo clima y la habitabilidad del planeta.
articles.image_prompt: A warm-toned, detailed painting of a layered Martian crater landscape showing distinct strata with varying textures and colors symbolizing different elevations, overlaid with delicate, crystalline iron oxide structures—small, intricate hematite crystallites near the top layers and larger, more developed ones deeper down—while a stylized robotic arm gently collects powdered rock samples beside a softly glowing, abstract representation of the Curiosity rover’s CheMin instrument analyzing minerals.

articles.title: Contractor to Civil Servant: NASA Welcomes Kenny Heckle
articles.summary: Kenny Heckle grew up in Orlando, just west of NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida. An 80s child, he comes from a long line of union pipefitters and fabricators. Heckle recalls the day 42 years ago at KARS Park, which is a NASA Exchange–run recreation area for the agency’s workforce and their guests, when he […]
articles.content:
Kenny Heckle, lead, mechanical operations, Launch Equipment Test Facility at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida, poses for a photograph on Monday, May 26, 2026. Heckle is among the first engineers and technicians sworn in as new NASA civil servants as part of the administrator’s workforce directive to restore technical core competencies within the civil servants ranks. Credit: NASA/Ben Smegelsky

Kenny Heckle grew up in Orlando, just west of NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida. An 80s child, he comes from a long line of union pipefitters and fabricators.

Heckle recalls the day 42 years ago at KARS Park, which is a NASA Exchange–run recreation area for the agency’s workforce and their guests, when he attended an office party with his father. Heckle had his German Shepherd with him when a man who seemed to be enamored with the canine asked him who he was. “I’m Kenny Heckle, Wayne’s son,” he said. And the man who knew his dad well replied, “Why don’t you work for us (at NASA)?”

Two weeks later, Heckle was working at the center alongside his dad.

Heckle wasn’t a typical new employee. At 19, he already had seven years of mechanical experience, working on his father’s short-track stock cars, building and fabricating parts they needed. He later attended welding school before arriving for his first job as a contractor at NASA Kennedy’s Launch Equipment Test Facility (LETF) in 1984.

Since the 1970s, the LETF has provided NASA a place to safely assess machinery and designs to support launches through a unique set of structures, equipment, and tools to test full-scale umbilicals and release mechanisms.

Today, Heckle serves as the mechanical operations lead at NASA Kennedy’s LETF.

During the past four decades, Heckle has helped numerous NASA programs and commercial partners test their equipment ahead of launch, and in some instances, during and after liftoff. In his early years, his job was to test every umbilical on the launch pad and all the ground support equipment needed for Launch Complex 39A and B, even for Vandenberg Space Force Base in California.

Just two years into his career, Space Shuttle Challenger had a failure of the O-ring seals and broke apart just over a minute into its flight. Heckle remembered watching the catastrophic liftoff that morning, and hearing the broadcaster say Challenger was lost. A couple of weeks later, his team was tasked with helping to figure out what happened.

“You know, there’s always risk with spaceflight,” Heckle said. “But we got so consistent that we didn’t think something like that could happen and it hit hard. But then being able to come back and get the program going again, and being successful, that makes you proud.”

Nearly two decades later, Heckle’s team was asked to help with yet another investigation. After the Colombia accident, Heckle and his team were charged with showing how severe the damage was through their testing, and how to mitigate ice hitting a wing in the future. They spent hours shooting projectiles at thermal tiles, using ultrasonic sensors to track the data.

In recent years, Heckle has helped work on the first two Artemis missions. During the Artemis II wet dress rehearsal, there was a liquid hydrogen leak. Heckle was working long days, troubleshooting and fabricating possible solutions with Kennedy’s Prototype Lab. For Artemis I they had a similar leak, and Heckle’s team developed a process to slow fill the cryogenics and the LETF sent that information to the Artemis I launch team to implement.

During decades of problem-solving, Heckle and most of his team were contractors, having to work through the bureaucracy of working solutions across different contractors, as well as with NASA. On May 4, Heckle and 19 of his teammates applied and became NASA civil servants as part of the administrator’s workforce directive. The work done by the LETF team was deemed a critical capability to NASA’s future, and as such, the work was moved from an outside vendor to civil service, ensuring NASA is staffed and equipped to lead the most complex engineering and operational challenges directly.

The test facility ensures NASA retains the technical readiness, flexibility, and risk mitigation capabilities required for Artemis, SLS (Space Launch System), and future government and commercial missions. As the mechanical operations lead, Heckle has already noticed efficiencies with being able to get work done and securing the supplies needed now the LETF team has joined the civil servant workforce.

“If we continue to work together as a team and not have barriers, I think that will be great for the program moving forward no matter what we’re launching,” Heckle said.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant positive development at NASA where critical technical expertise and operations at the Launch Equipment Test Facility have been transitioned from contractors to civil servants. This move ensures NASA retains essential technical readiness and operational capabilities for major space missions like Artemis and SLS, which has broad significance for space exploration and national space leadership. The story provides detailed context about the individual's long-term contribution and the facility's role, showing tangible benefits to NASA's mission success and workforce stability.
articles.personality_title: NASA welcomes longtime technician Kenny Heckle as civil servant to strengthen launch testing
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Kenny Heckle has worked at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida for over 40 years, starting as a contractor at the Launch Equipment Test Facility (LETF). This facility tests important launch equipment to keep space missions safe.

**What happened** – On May 4, 2026, Kenny Heckle and 19 of his teammates became official NASA civil servants. This change was part of NASA’s plan to have key technical jobs handled directly by government employees instead of contractors.

**Impact** – Moving the LETF team from contractors to civil servants helps NASA keep strong technical skills in-house. This is important for missions like Artemis and the Space Launch System (SLS), which need careful testing to succeed. Heckle’s experience, from testing launch equipment to solving problems during past accidents and recent Artemis missions, shows how valuable this expertise is.

**What's next step** – As civil servants, Heckle and his team will have better access to resources and can work more smoothly with NASA programs. This should make future mission preparations faster and more reliable.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s decision to hire key technicians like Kenny Heckle as civil servants strengthens its ability to prepare safely for future space launches.
articles.personality_title_fr: NASA accueille Kenny Heckle, technicien de longue date, comme fonctionnaire pour renforcer les tests de lancement
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Kenny Heckle travaille au Centre spatial Kennedy de la NASA en Floride depuis plus de 40 ans, d’abord comme contractant au Launch Equipment Test Facility (LETF). Ce centre teste des équipements importants pour assurer la sécurité des missions spatiales.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 4 mai 2026, Kenny Heckle et 19 de ses collègues sont devenus fonctionnaires de la NASA. Cette décision fait partie du plan de la NASA pour que les emplois techniques clés soient assurés directement par des employés gouvernementaux plutôt que par des contractants.

**Impact** – Le passage de l’équipe LETF de contractants à fonctionnaires permet à la NASA de conserver ses compétences techniques en interne. C’est important pour des missions comme Artemis et le Space Launch System (SLS), qui nécessitent des tests rigoureux pour réussir. L’expérience de Heckle, qui a travaillé sur les équipements de lancement et aidé après des accidents passés ainsi que sur les missions Artemis récentes, montre la valeur de cette expertise.

**Prochaine étape** – En tant que fonctionnaires, Heckle et son équipe auront un meilleur accès aux ressources et pourront collaborer plus facilement avec les programmes de la NASA. Cela devrait rendre les préparations des futures missions plus rapides et fiables.

**Phrase clé** – La décision de la NASA d’embaucher des techniciens clés comme Kenny Heckle en tant que fonctionnaires renforce sa capacité à préparer en toute sécurité les futurs lancements spatiaux.
articles.personality_title_es: NASA incorpora al veterano técnico Kenny Heckle como empleado civil para fortalecer las pruebas de lanzamiento
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Kenny Heckle ha trabajado en el Centro Espacial Kennedy de la NASA en Florida durante más de 40 años, comenzando como contratista en el Launch Equipment Test Facility (LETF). Esta instalación prueba equipos importantes para mantener seguras las misiones espaciales.

**Qué pasó** – El 4 de mayo de 2026, Kenny Heckle y 19 de sus compañeros se convirtieron en empleados civiles oficiales de la NASA. Este cambio forma parte del plan de la NASA para que los trabajos técnicos clave sean manejados directamente por empleados gubernamentales en lugar de contratistas.

**Impacto** – Pasar al equipo LETF de contratistas a empleados civiles ayuda a la NASA a mantener fuertes habilidades técnicas internas. Esto es importante para misiones como Artemis y el Sistema de Lanzamiento Espacial (SLS), que necesitan pruebas cuidadosas para tener éxito. La experiencia de Heckle, desde probar equipos de lanzamiento hasta resolver problemas tras accidentes pasados y en misiones recientes de Artemis, muestra cuán valiosa es esta experiencia.

**Próximo paso** – Como empleados civiles, Heckle y su equipo tendrán mejor acceso a recursos y podrán trabajar más fácilmente con los programas de la NASA. Esto debería hacer que la preparación de futuras misiones sea más rápida y confiable.

**Frase para llevar** – La decisión de la NASA de contratar técnicos clave como Kenny Heckle como empleados civiles fortalece su capacidad para preparar con seguridad futuros lanzamientos espaciales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a sturdy mechanical gear entwined with a soaring rocket silhouette rising above a stylized launch pad structure, set against a soft sky of natural blues and gentle earth tones, symbolizing enduring dedication, teamwork, and the transition from contractor to civil servant at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center.

articles.title: Millions of planets might form around supermassive black holes
articles.summary: Massive amounts of dust swirl around active nuclei at the centres of galaxies, and these discs could give rise to vast numbers of rocky planets, some even the size of stars
articles.content:
Massive amounts of dust swirl around active nuclei at the centres of galaxies, and these discs could give rise to vast numbers of rocky planets, some even the size of stars

The disc of material swirling around a supermassive black hole may give birth to many planets NASA and M. Weiss/Chandra X-ray Center

The active centres of galaxies might be regions of extraordinary planet formation, where millions of worlds are born.

Most galaxies in the universe, such as our own Milky Way, host a supermassive black hole at their centre. Most of the time, these black holes are quiescent, as there is no matter falling into them. But occasionally they become active and consume huge amounts of dust and gas, perhaps from a merger with another galaxy, becoming an active galactic nucleus for millions of years.

Barry McKernan at the City University of New York and his colleagues modelled the disc of dust and gas around a typical active galactic nucleus. They found it would be a prime location for planet formation, with the dust easily clumping together into bigger and bigger objects. Eventually planets would begin to grow in huge numbers, and with strange properties.

“This is a really amazing new pathway to form very alien planets,” says McKernan. “If these things exist, they’re quite unlike planets that we know and love.”

The planets would grow to enormous sizes because active galactic nuclei contain massive amounts of dust, much more than the protoplanetary discs around young stars that formed solar systems like our own. That could lead to giant rocky planets the size of Jupiter or even bigger forming, something not known to happen anywhere else in the universe, many with surfaces covered in lava because of frequent collisions with other worlds.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

Some of the planets would become so large that they could ignite nuclear fusion at their cores, says McKernan, becoming “very weird alien stars” made of rock, or swallow up large amounts of nearby gas and collapse into objects known as intermediate-mass black holes.

The disc of dust around an active galactic nucleus can extend for dozens of light years, meaning this process would take place at huge scales. “You could get millions of planets around the central supermassive black hole,” says McKernan.

We knew that planets and stars can form around black holes, but planet formation of this scale has not been investigated before, says Sean Raymond at the University of Bordeaux, France. It could make active supermassive black holes one of the best places in the universe to form new worlds.

“With that much stuff around a supermassive black hole, what else is going to happen?” says Raymond. “It seems pretty much unavoidable.”

Many of the planets would be scattered into the black hole or ejected out into the galaxy because of their repeated interactions with each other. Any that remain might be detectable, perhaps by noticing their gravity warp the light of more distant stars, a technique called microlensing.

Telescopes such as NASA’s Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope, which is expected to launch in September, could make this possible. “We are going into the age when microlensing is very much a thing,” says Benne Holwerda at the University of Louisville, Kentucky.

McKernan also notes that many active galactic nuclei have been observed to flicker, which could be due to a “swarm of little things that are passing in front”, such as planets. “These things should exist,” says McKernan. “So can we see them?”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about the potential for massive planet formation around supermassive black holes, which expands our understanding of planetary systems and the universe. This breakthrough has broad implications for astronomy and space science, inspiring curiosity and hope about new worlds and cosmic phenomena. The article provides detailed context about the process, scale, and potential for future detection, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Millions of giant planets may form around supermassive black holes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Most galaxies, including our Milky Way, have a supermassive black hole at their center. Sometimes these black holes become active by pulling in large amounts of dust and gas, creating a bright and powerful area called an active galactic nucleus.

**What happened** – Scientists led by Barry McKernan studied the dust and gas around these active black holes. They found that this swirling disc can clump together to form millions of rocky planets, some even larger than Jupiter. Some planets might grow so big they become unusual star-like objects or collapse into smaller black holes.

**Impact** – This discovery shows a new way planets can form in the universe, different from how planets like Earth are made around stars. It means that active black holes could be home to vast numbers of strange and huge worlds. This idea changes how we think about where planets can exist and how big they can get.

**What's next step** – Scientists hope to detect these planets by using a method called microlensing, which looks for how these planets bend light from distant stars. New telescopes, such as NASA’s Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope launching soon, might help find these planets and confirm their existence.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Active supermassive black holes may create millions of enormous and unusual planets, opening a new chapter in our understanding of the universe.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des millions de planètes géantes pourraient se former autour des trous noirs supermassifs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La plupart des galaxies, comme la Voie lactée, possèdent un trou noir supermassif en leur centre. Parfois, ces trous noirs deviennent actifs en attirant de grandes quantités de poussière et de gaz, créant une zone brillante et puissante appelée noyau actif de galaxie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques dirigés par Barry McKernan ont étudié la poussière et le gaz autour de ces trous noirs actifs. Ils ont découvert que ce disque en rotation peut s’agglomérer pour former des millions de planètes rocheuses, certaines même plus grandes que Jupiter. Certaines planètes pourraient devenir si grosses qu’elles ressemblent à des étoiles étranges ou s’effondrer en petits trous noirs.

**Impact** – Cette découverte révèle une nouvelle façon dont les planètes peuvent se former dans l’univers, différente de la formation des planètes comme la Terre autour des étoiles. Cela signifie que les trous noirs actifs pourraient abriter un grand nombre de mondes étranges et énormes. Cette idée change notre vision des endroits où les planètes peuvent exister et de leur taille possible.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent détecter ces planètes grâce à une méthode appelée microlentille gravitationnelle, qui observe comment ces planètes déforment la lumière des étoiles lointaines. De nouveaux télescopes, comme le Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope de la NASA qui sera bientôt lancé, pourraient aider à les trouver et à confirmer leur existence.

**Phrase clé** – Les trous noirs supermassifs actifs pourraient créer des millions de planètes énormes et inhabituelles, ouvrant un nouveau chapitre dans notre compréhension de l’univers.
articles.personality_title_es: Millones de planetas gigantes podrían formarse alrededor de agujeros negros supermasivos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La mayoría de las galaxias, incluida nuestra Vía Láctea, tienen un agujero negro supermasivo en su centro. A veces, estos agujeros negros se vuelven activos al atraer grandes cantidades de polvo y gas, creando una zona brillante y poderosa llamada núcleo galáctico activo.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos liderados por Barry McKernan estudiaron el polvo y gas alrededor de estos agujeros negros activos. Descubrieron que este disco giratorio puede juntarse para formar millones de planetas rocosos, algunos incluso más grandes que Júpiter. Algunos planetas podrían crecer tanto que se vuelvan objetos extraños parecidos a estrellas o colapsen en pequeños agujeros negros.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra una nueva forma en que los planetas pueden formarse en el universo, diferente a cómo se forman planetas como la Tierra alrededor de estrellas. Esto significa que los agujeros negros activos podrían ser el hogar de muchos mundos extraños y enormes. Esta idea cambia cómo pensamos dónde pueden existir los planetas y qué tamaño pueden alcanzar.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan detectar estos planetas usando un método llamado microlente gravitacional, que observa cómo estos planetas doblan la luz de estrellas lejanas. Nuevos telescopios, como el Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope de la NASA, que será lanzado pronto, podrían ayudar a encontrarlos y confirmar su existencia.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los agujeros negros supermasivos activos pueden crear millones de planetas enormes y extraños, abriendo un nuevo capítulo en nuestra comprensión del universo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vast, glowing swirling disc of dust and gas encircling a dark, enigmatic supermassive black hole at the center of a galaxy, with numerous large, rocky planets—some glowing softly with molten lava surfaces—forming and orbiting within the disc, all rendered in gentle, natural earth tones and deep cosmic blues.
